name: Kaza
tagline: The only place in Spiti with an ATM, a petrol pump, and a prayer wheel — your base camp for everything.
why_visit: Kaza is not a destination, it's a launchpad. The town itself is functional — dusty main road, a few cafes that serve decent momos and terrible coffee. But you'll keep coming back here between day trips because it's the only place with reliable beds, the only ATM for 200km, and the last petrol pump if you're headed deeper. The old town (Kaza Soma) has more character than the new bazaar side.
highlights: Base for all Spiti exploration — every village is a day trip from here
Sakya Tangyud Monastery in old Kaza
The only ATM and fuel station in the entire valley (carry cash anyway, it's often empty)
Cafe Sol and Himalayan Cafe for decent food by Spiti standards
Night sky from the outskirts — zero light pollution
kids_note: 3650m is serious altitude for kids under 10. AMS risk is real. If you must bring kids, acclimatize for 2 full days minimum before doing anything. No pediatric medical care available — nearest hospital is in Reckong Peo (10+ hours).
time_needed: 2-3 days as a base (you'll sleep here but spend days elsewhere)

name: Key Monastery
tagline: The postcard image of Spiti — a thousand-year-old gompa stacked like boxes on a hilltop, completely alone against the sky.
why_visit: Key (or Ki or Kye) Gompa is the largest and most iconic monastery in Spiti. It's been destroyed by Mongols, Sikhs, earthquakes, and fire over its 1000+ year life — and rebuilt every time. The building itself looks like it was designed by Escher, rooms stacked on rooms with no apparent logic. About 300 monks live here. The prayer hall has genuinely stunning murals, and the view from the roof terrace is the single most photographed frame in all of Spiti. Come at sunrise or sunset, not midday.
highlights: Sunrise light hitting the monastery from across the valley — the shot everyone comes for
Ancient wall murals and thangka paintings inside the prayer hall
Morning and evening prayer sessions (visitors welcome, be respectful and silent)
Rooftop terrace view of the Spiti River valley
Annual Cham dance festival (usually June/July, dates follow Tibetan calendar)
kids_note: 4166m elevation. Steep climb up to the monastery with no railing in places. Not suitable for young children — both the altitude and the physical access are problems.

name: Kibber
tagline: Perched at 4270m with snow leopards in the backyard — Kibber is where Spiti gets properly wild.
why_visit: Kibber used to claim 'highest village with a road' before everyone else started claiming the same thing. What actually makes it special: it sits inside a wildlife sanctuary that's one of the best places on Earth to spot snow leopards in winter. In summer, it's a high-altitude village with stone houses, blue sheep on the ridges, and homestays run by families who've lived here for centuries. The Kibber-Chicham bridge (Asia's highest suspension bridge, if you believe the claim) is nearby.
highlights: Snow leopard tracking expeditions (January-February, with local operators)
Kibber Wildlife Sanctuary — blue sheep (bharal), Himalayan ibex, golden eagle
Chicham Bridge — suspension bridge over a terrifying gorge
Some of the best homestays in Spiti (try Norling Homestay)
Starting point for the Parang La trek to Ladakh
kids_note: 4270m — firmly in the danger zone for altitude sickness in children. Steep drops around the village with no barriers. Snow leopard treks are multi-day and physically demanding. Not a kids' destination.
time_needed: Half day visit, or 2-3 days if doing snow leopard tracking

name: Langza
tagline: A giant Buddha watches over 100 people and a million fossils — Langza is Spiti distilled to its essence.
why_visit: Langza has about 130 residents, a massive Buddha statue overlooking the valley, and the ground is literally made of fossils. This was the bed of the Tethys Sea millions of years ago, and you'll find marine fossils (ammonites, trilobites, sea lilies) just lying in the dirt. The village is split into Langza Yongma (lower) and Langza Gongma (upper). The upper village has a small monastery with ancient murals. The sense of scale here — tiny village, giant peaks, enormous sky — is what breaks people's brains about Spiti.
highlights: Fossil hunting — marine fossils scattered across the hillside (look, don't take)
The giant golden Buddha statue with Chau Chau Kang Nilda (6303m) behind it
Langza Monastery — tiny, ancient, rarely visited
One of the best sunset viewpoints in all of Spiti
Homestay dinners — local barley dishes, butter tea, thukpa
kids_note: 4400m — too high for children. The fossil hunting sounds kid-friendly but the altitude is not. Serious AMS risk.
time_needed: 3-4 hours (half day with travel from Kaza)

name: Hikkim
tagline: The world's highest post office. Yes, it actually works. Send a postcard, it'll arrive in 3 weeks.
why_visit: Hikkim is famous for exactly one thing: its post office at 4440m, claimed to be the highest in the world. It's a tiny room in a stone building, run by a postmaster who commutes from Kaza. You buy a postcard, write it, get it stamped with the special Hikkim cancellation mark, and it genuinely gets delivered — eventually. That's the whole attraction, and honestly, it's enough. The village itself is about 50 houses and a small monastery. Usually combined with Langza and Komic as a day trip from Kaza.
highlights: Send a postcard from the world's highest post office (bring your own pen)
Special Hikkim postal cancellation stamp — a genuine collectible
Combine with Langza and Komic for the classic Spiti village triangle day trip
Small monastery with prayer flags against massive peaks
The road itself between Hikkim, Langza, and Komic is absurdly scenic
kids_note: 4440m. Same altitude concerns as Langza and Komic. Sending a postcard sounds like a cute kid activity but the altitude makes it irresponsible for young children.
time_needed: 30 minutes to 1 hour (usually part of a combined village loop)

name: Komic
tagline: Claims to be the highest motorable village in Asia. The claim is disputed. The views are not.
why_visit: Komic's claim to fame — 'highest village in the world connected by a motorable road' — has been challenged by about fifteen other villages. Doesn't matter. What matters: Tangyud Monastery here is a proper working gompa with monks, prayer sessions, and wall paintings that rival Key's. The village has maybe 30 houses. The landscape around it is pure moonscape — bare brown ridges, zero trees, prayer flags snapping in a wind that never stops. Komic is where you realize how extreme life in Spiti actually is.
highlights: Tangyud Monastery — 14th century, active, impressive murals
Fossil-rich terrain similar to Langza
The drive up from Kaza — switchbacks through completely barren landscape
Prayer flag-draped pass on the approach road
Combined day trip with Hikkim and Langza (the 'high village triangle')
kids_note: 4587m — the highest point on the village circuit. Absolutely not for children. Even fit adults feel the altitude here.

name: Dhankar
tagline: A crumbling fort-monastery on a cliff edge, and a turquoise lake hidden behind the ridge. Dhankar earns its spot.
why_visit: Dhankar was the ancient capital of Spiti. The old monastery-fort complex clings to a crumbling cliff face — it's been on the World Monuments Fund's 'most endangered' list, and you can see why. One look at it and you wonder how it's still standing. The new monastery nearby is functional but less dramatic. The real prize is Dhankar Lake — a 1-hour hike up behind the ridge, a small turquoise pool reflecting massive peaks. The confluence of Spiti and Pin rivers visible from the monastery is one of the valley's most photographed views.
highlights: The old fort-monastery perched impossibly on eroding cliffs
Dhankar Lake hike — 1 hour up, turquoise water, massive peaks reflected
Confluence of Spiti and Pin rivers visible from the monastery viewpoint
Ancient murals and statues inside the old gompa (ask for permission)
Sunrise from the ridge above the monastery
kids_note: The old monastery has crumbling edges with sheer drops and no railings whatsoever. The lake hike is at 4100m+ on loose scree. Both are genuinely dangerous for children.

name: Tabo
tagline: Founded in 996 CE. Over a thousand years of unbroken Buddhist practice. Sometimes called the Ajanta of the Himalayas — and it's not hyperbole.
why_visit: Tabo Monastery is the real deal — founded in 996 CE, it's one of the oldest continuously operating Buddhist monasteries in the world. The Dalai Lama has called it his favourite. Inside: 9 temples with murals, stucco sculptures, and thangkas that rival anything in Ladakh or Tibet. The art is genuinely world-class — UNESCO-worthy, still unprotected. The cave hermitages in the cliff above the monastery were used by monks for meditation for centuries. Tabo itself is a sleepy town at a relatively lower altitude (3280m), making it a decent acclimatization stop.
highlights: The Tsuklakhang (main prayer hall) — 1000-year-old murals and stucco art
Cave hermitages carved into the cliff face above the monastery
Significantly lower altitude than upper Spiti — good acclimatization stop
The Dalai Lama's own designated meditation chamber
One of the few places in Spiti where you might get phone signal (BSNL)
kids_note: At 3280m, Tabo is one of the lower-altitude stops in Spiti — still high, but manageable for older children (10+) who've been acclimatizing. The monastery itself is flat and walkable. The cave hermitages require a short climb. Better than most Spiti stops for families, but still not casual.

name: Pin Valley National Park
tagline: Snow leopard country. One of India's highest national parks, and one of the emptiest — you might be the only visitor for days.
why_visit: Pin Valley is where Spiti's wildlife lives. The national park is home to snow leopards, ibex, bharal (blue sheep), Tibetan wolf, and a surprising number of bird species. The valley runs from Dhankar to Mud village, following the Pin River through a narrow gorge that opens into wide meadows. In summer, the valley floor is green with wild roses and medicinal herbs. Kungri Monastery (one of the oldest Nyingmapa gompas in Spiti) sits at the entrance. The park is rarely crowded — most Spiti visitors skip it, which is exactly why you shouldn't.
highlights: Snow leopard habitat — sightings possible in winter with operator
Kungri Monastery — rare Nyingmapa (oldest Tibetan Buddhist school) gompa
Pin-Bhaba Pass trek (challenging, connects to Kinnaur)
Wild flowers and medicinal herb meadows in July-August
Bharal (blue sheep) herds visible from the road
kids_note: Remote, high altitude, poor road conditions. No medical facilities anywhere in the valley. Wildlife treks involve long walks at 4000m+. Not recommended for children.

name: Chandratal Lake
tagline: A crescent moon lake at 4300m that changes colour every hour. Camp here under the Milky Way and you'll understand why people cry at nature.
why_visit: Chandratal (Moon Lake) is a high-altitude lake that shifts between deep blue, emerald green, and turquoise depending on the light and time of day. It sits in a bowl surrounded by barren mountains, about 14km off the Manali-Kaza highway near Kunzum Pass. The camping experience here is the highlight — fall asleep to absolute silence, wake up to stars so dense they look fake. The lake is small (about 2.5km circumference) but the setting is staggering. No permanent structures allowed, only seasonal tent camps. This is Spiti's single most popular overnight experience.
highlights: The lake itself — colour-shifting, crescent-shaped, impossibly clear
Overnight camping under what might be India's best night sky
Short trek (1km) from the parking area to the lakeshore
Kunzum Pass (4590m) on the approach — prayer flags and panoramic views
Dawn light on the lake is when the colour changes are most dramatic
kids_note: 4300m with overnight camping in basic tents. Temperature drops to near-zero at night even in summer. No toilets, no medical help, no phone signal. The approach road is one of the worst in Spiti. Hard no for kids.

name: Gette
tagline: Twelve houses on a cliff in a cold desert. Gette is what happens when humans decide to live somewhere they really shouldn't.
why_visit: Gette is one of Spiti's tiniest settlements — about 12 houses clinging to a hillside above the Spiti River. There's no 'attraction' here in the tourist sense. The attraction is the place itself: the absurd drama of this tiny cluster of whitewashed houses against a backdrop of eroded, layered rock formations that look like a geology textbook illustration. It's the kind of place where you stand there for ten minutes trying to understand how anyone chose to build a village here. Most visitors pass it on the road between Kaza and Dhankar without stopping. Stop.
highlights: One of the most dramatically situated villages in the entire Himalaya
Eroded cliff formations that look like a different planet
No tourists — you'll likely be the only visitor
Quick stop between Kaza and Dhankar/Tabo
Photography gold — the scale of landscape vs village is staggering
kids_note: Steep village paths with no barriers, crumbling cliffs. Not inherently dangerous for a quick roadside photo stop, but don't let kids wander.

name: Demul
tagline: Off the grid in the most literal sense. No shops, no signal, no agenda. Just a village living the way it has for centuries.
why_visit: Demul is what people imagine when they think of 'remote Himalayan village' — except it's actually like that. About 30 houses, no shops, no phone signal, no road until recently (the track is still barely drivable). The homestays here are run by families who farm barley, herd yaks, and spin wool. You eat what they eat, sleep when they sleep, wake up to mountains. The village sits on a plateau with 360-degree mountain views. It's one of the few places in Spiti where tourism hasn't changed anything yet, mostly because it's genuinely hard to get to.
highlights: Authentic homestay experience — the real thing, not performative
360-degree mountain panorama from the village plateau
Zero phone signal, zero shops, zero tourists most days
Traditional Spitian agriculture — watch barley harvesting, yak herding
One of the starting points for the Demul-Lidang-Dhankar trek
kids_note: 4270m, extremely remote, no medical access, rough access road. The village itself is gentle and safe but the logistics make it unsuitable for children.
time_needed: Overnight (homestay) to get the real experience

name: Lhalung
tagline: The golden temple of Spiti, hidden in a village most tourists never reach. Lhalung's monastery murals rival Tabo's — without the crowds.
why_visit: Lhalung Monastery (Serkhang — literally 'golden temple') contains some of the oldest and most extraordinary Buddhist art in the western Himalaya. The gilded stucco panels and painted wooden ceilings here are arguably more impressive than Tabo's, yet barely anyone visits because the village is off the main road. The monastery dates to the 10th-11th century. Lhalung village itself is beautiful — terraced fields, apricot trees, traditional houses. This is the insider's pick in Spiti, the place that people who've been three times tell you about.
highlights: Serkhang (Golden Temple) — 10th century gilded stucco panels, jaw-dropping
Ancient wooden ceilings with painted Buddhist iconography
Apricot orchards and terraced barley fields in the village
Almost zero tourists — this is the Spiti that locals are proud of
Side trip from the Dhankar-Tabo road
kids_note: At 3658m, slightly higher than Tabo but still in the more manageable lower Spiti range. The village is flat and gentle. Older kids (10+) who have acclimatized can handle it. The monastery requires respectful quiet.

name: Mud Village
tagline: The road ends here. Literally. Last village in Pin Valley, first village in nowhere.
why_visit: Mud is the last inhabited village in Pin Valley — the road just stops. Beyond it is wilderness, the Pin-Parvati Pass, and eventually Kullu (if you're a serious trekker). The village is the gateway for the Pin-Bhaba and Pin-Parvati treks, but even if you're not trekking, the drive through Pin Valley gorge to reach Mud is one of the most dramatic road journeys in Spiti. The village has a handful of homestays and a small monastery. The feeling of being at the end of a road, surrounded by 5000m+ peaks, with nowhere further to drive — that's the point.
highlights: End-of-the-road feeling — the last settlement before true wilderness
Gateway for Pin-Parvati Pass trek (one of India's classic high-altitude crossings)
The drive through Pin Valley gorge — narrow, dramatic, barely a road
Small village monastery with friendly monks
Homestays with families who farm in genuinely extreme conditions
kids_note: Remote, rough road, 3770m altitude, no medical facilities. The Pin Valley road itself is narrow with drops. Not for kids.
time_needed: Overnight if trekking, 3-4 hours for a day visit
